section_1_metadata.note_on_delay: Report for year ended December 2019 was not signed until August 2020 — an 8-month delay. COVID-19 cited. This is a Vitics signal: the sector was so stressed that basic governance timelines were collapsing.
section_1_metadata.auditor: KPMG Professional Services, Victoria Island Lagos
section_1_metadata.auditor_tenure_note: KPMG's final year as auditor. CAMA audit tenure rules triggered compulsory rotation after this report.
section_1_metadata.parent_company_note: KANN is a joint venture between Xerxes Global Investment Ltd and CEC Africa Investments Ltd. KANN entered receivership January 2020 — see Section 16.

revenue_breakdown.note: 164.9 billion of the 256 billion revenue is a NERC regulatory award — not real cash collected from consumers. Actual electricity sold = 88.3 billion. Without the NERC award, AEDC would have reported a massive loss for the seventh consecutive year.

section_2_income_statement.vitics_note_on_profit: The profit of NGN124.3 billion is almost entirely accounting paper. NERC awarded tariff shortfalls of NGN164.9 billion for years 2015-2019, offset against NBET payables rather than paid in cash. Strip that out and AEDC lost money again. Consumers got nothing from this 'profit.'

section_3_balance_sheet.equity_improvement_note: Equity improved from negative NGN128.8 billion to negative NGN4.6 billion — almost entirely due to NERC tariff shortfall awards, not operational improvement.
section_3_balance_sheet.current_ratio: 50.9 billion assets vs 145.4 billion liabilities — current liabilities exceed current assets by NGN94.5 billion. The company cannot pay its short-term bills.

section_4_cash_flow.cash_collapse_note: Cash fell from NGN4.4 billion to NGN1.3 billion — a 71% reduction in one year. The company is running dry. Despite booking a NGN124 billion 'profit', actual cash on hand is barely NGN1.3 billion.
section_4_cash_flow.capex_note: Capex declining: NGN3.7bn in 2019 vs NGN4.7bn in 2018. Infrastructure investment is shrinking as the company runs out of real cash.

electricity_sales_by_payment_mode.prepaid_growth_note: Prepaid revenue grew from NGN16.9bn to NGN22.8bn — a 35% increase. More consumers switching to prepaid as trust in postpaid billing collapses. Vitics signal: prepaid shift = consumers seizing control of their own electricity consumption.

tariff_shortfall_detail.settlement_method: All netted off against NBET payables — zero cash changed hands
tariff_shortfall_detail.vitics_meaning: NERC awarded 4+ years of accumulated shortfalls in one shot to prevent AEDC from collapsing. This is a sector-wide triage operation, not financial health. The regulatory body is effectively keeping distribution companies on life support.

customer_contributed_assets.MAP_regulation_note: NERC's Meter Asset Provider (MAP) regulation requires customers to pay for their own meters through third-party providers. AEDC appointed 3 MAPs. Customers bear all delivery, activation and maintenance costs, yet AEDC owns the meters. Consumer funds infrastructure again — same pattern as CAPMI in 2015.

section_5_revenue_analysis.revenue_recognition_key_risk: 57% of AEDC customers are unmetered — bills estimated per feeder using load wattage calculations. This is not real measurement. Unmetered customers in the lowest tier had a 71% loss rate on receivables.

section_6_receivables_and_collection.impairment_coverage_ratio: 66% of gross receivables already written off as uncollectable

ecl_by_customer_segment.unmetered_non_MD_low_tier_note: NGN93.2 billion gross, NGN67.1 billion written off. This is the mass residential consumer segment — ordinary Nigerians receiving estimated bills they cannot pay. The largest receivables balance and the highest loss rate.
ecl_by_customer_segment.prepaid_loss_note: Even prepaid customers show 49% loss — these are legacy postpaid debts from customers who migrated to prepaid. The debt followed them.
ecl_by_customer_segment.metered_MD_note: Commercial/industrial customers pay best — 11% loss rate. The clearest evidence that the collection crisis is concentrated among residential consumers.

section_6_receivables_and_collection.impairment_trend_note: Impairment charge growing every year: NGN18.3bn (2015), NGN19.2bn (2018), NGN21.9bn (2019). The collection problem is getting worse, not better, six years after privatisation.
section_6_receivables_and_collection.nbet_reduction_note: NBET payables fell from NGN235bn to NGN103bn because of the NERC shortfall netting, not because AEDC paid its bills. Without the regulatory award, AEDC would owe NGN265+ billion to NBET.
section_6_receivables_and_collection.minimum_remittance_requirement: AEDC required to remit 45% of NBET bills and 100% of ONEM bills under NERC order. Non-payment attracts NIBOR+4% interest.
section_6_receivables_and_collection.license_cancellation_notice: In October 2019, NERC issued notice of intention to cancel AEDC's licence for failure to meet 45% remittance threshold. AEDC showed cause. Resolved by December 2019 NERC order acknowledging compliance. The threat was real.

section_7_cost_analysis.OM_fee_note: Operations and Management fee paid to parent KANN at 2% of net cash collected. NGN1.73bn paid to foreign-controlled parent while the company cannot pay its electricity bills. Consumer funds flow offshore.
section_7_cost_analysis.security_cost_note: NGN913 million on security — a distribution company spending nearly a billion naira protecting its infrastructure. Reflects the operating environment.
section_7_cost_analysis.vendor_commission_note: Super vendor commissions 0.5-4.25% of sales for online vending platforms — consumers pay the commission indirectly.

CBN_NEMSF_detail.note: CBN's market stabilisation fund is the only thing keeping AEDC operational. Without government intervention money, the company collapses. This is not a private sector electricity system — it is a government-subsidised operation with private ownership capturing management fees.

letter_of_credit.purpose: Required for participation in Transitional Electricity Market (TEM)

CAPMI_scheme.nature: Consumer-funded meter loans — consumers advanced funds at 12% nominal interest, repayable over 3 years via electricity units. Scheme abolished but residual debt remains.

section_9_ppe_and_infrastructure.capex_vs_depreciation_note: Capex NGN3.7bn vs depreciation NGN4.5bn — the company is consuming assets faster than it is replacing them. Net infrastructure is declining in real terms.
section_9_ppe_and_infrastructure.NIPP_assets: Six injection substations from NDPHC energised in 2018, fair value NGN1.64bn. Government infrastructure handed to a private operator.
section_9_ppe_and_infrastructure.last_revaluation: Distribution network assets revalued 31 December 2018 by external valuers using depreciated replacement cost.

section_10_metering.metering_situation_2019: Majority of AEDC customers remain unmetered. Bills estimated per feeder using load wattage calculations.
section_10_metering.MAP_regulation: NERC issued Meter Asset Provider (MAP) regulation. Third-party meter companies (MAPs) procure and install meters. Customers bear all costs.
section_10_metering.meter_cost_to_consumers: Customers bear delivery, activation, and maintenance costs. AEDC owns the meters. Customer funds AEDC's asset base.
section_10_metering.CAPMI_abolished: CAPMI scheme abolished 2016. MAP is the replacement — structurally similar in that consumers still fund meters.
section_10_metering.InCMS_deployment: New Integrated Commercial Management System deployed in 2019 to unify postpaid and prepaid customer database.
section_10_metering.vitics_metering_signal: In 2019, AEDC is still heavily unmetered — MAP regulation is the third attempt (after CAPMI and direct billing) to solve metering by making consumers pay. Each new scheme maintains the same logic: consumer funds infrastructure.

section_11_going_concern.kpmg_qualification_text: Material uncertainty exists that may cast significant doubt on the Company's ability to continue as a going concern.

five_year_loss_record.2019_note: Reported profit of NGN124.3bn — but 97% of this is the NERC tariff shortfall award, not operational improvement

going_concern_dependencies: Continued receipt of NERC tariff shortfall awards
NBET/ONEM not calling due debts of NGN103 billion
Continued permission to pay only 42% of NBET bills
section_11_going_concern.PSRP_reference: Power Sector Recovery Program — FGN acknowledges DISCO illiquidity is caused by absence of cost-reflective tariffs.
section_11_going_concern.NBET_debt_restructuring_proposed: Discussions to convert NGN103bn NBET debt into 20-year loan. No approval obtained.
section_11_going_concern.service_reflective_tariff: Approved for implementation 1 July 2020 — then suspended by NERC due to COVID-19.
section_11_going_concern.vitics_meaning: AEDC has been technically insolvent every year since privatisation in 2013. The only thing keeping it operating is a combination of: (1) regulatory paper awards, (2) CBN intervention money, (3) forbearance from NBET, and (4) consumer-funded infrastructure. This is not a functioning electricity market — it is regulatory life support with private management fees.

section_12_regulatory_environment.MYTO_framework: Multi-Year Tariff Order — basis for all pricing. NERC issues minor reviews annually.
section_12_regulatory_environment.TEM_rules: Transitional Electricity Market — effective 1 February 2015, governs trading. AEDC required to remit 45% of NBET bills.
section_12_regulatory_environment.tariff_shortfall_mechanism: Gap between what consumers pay (regulated tariff) and what electricity actually costs (cost-reflective tariff) accumulated since 2015. NERC awarded NGN164.9bn in one block to AEDC in 2019.

cost_reflective_tariff_timeline.MYTO_original_promise: Cost-reflective tariffs to be achieved progressively
cost_reflective_tariff_timeline.2019_status: Still not achieved — shortfalls being awarded instead
cost_reflective_tariff_timeline.2020_attempt: Service reflective tariff approved July 2020 but suspended for COVID-19

NERC_orders_issued_2019: Order No. NERC/GL/170 — 2016-2018 Minor Review and 2019 Minimum Remittance
Order No. NERC/GL/184/2019 — December 2019 Minor Review and 2020 Minimum Remittance
October 2019 — Notice of intention to cancel AEDC licence (and 7 others) for under-remittance
December 2019 — Acknowledged AEDC showed cause; licence cancellation resolved
section_12_regulatory_environment.licence_cancellation_note: The threat of licence cancellation in October 2019 is significant — NERC is signalling that regulatory patience has limits. This was the first time the threat became public.

KANN_receivership_2020.event: KANN Utility Company Limited (60% owner of AEDC) entered receivership
KANN_receivership_2020.trigger: KANN unable to meet loan obligations to its banker
KANN_receivership_2020.effect: Federal High Court restrained KANN directors from dealing with assets
KANN_receivership_2020.injunction: CEC Africa Investments Limited filed interlocutory injunction to block receiver
KANN_receivership_2020.AEDC_directors_view: Debenture creating 60% charge on AEDC is void — maximum security amount already paid. Liquidation proceedings have no impact on AEDC.
KANN_receivership_2020.vitics_significance: AEDC's majority owner collapsed into receivership four months after this financial year ended. The 60% private owner of a utility serving FCT Abuja and 4 states went into receivership — this is the ultimate signal that the post-privatisation model had failed.

COVID_19_2020.AEDC_status: Classified as essential service — exempted from lockdown
COVID_19_2020.impact: Protracted fall in demand, slowdown in collections, aggravated liquidity shortfall
COVID_19_2020.tariff_consequence: Service reflective tariff suspended by NERC due to COVID-19 impact on economy

prior_force_majeure.KANN_force_majeure_2015: Previously declared force majeure April 2015 (withdrawn February 2016) — referenced in 2015 report
prior_force_majeure.2019_status: No active force majeure declared during 2019 financial year

section_14_cbn_intervention_fund.accounting_treatment: Below-market interest rate — day-one gain recognised as deferred income (government grant). NGN5.9bn of deferred income relates to day-one gains on CBN/TCN/NELMCO loans.
section_14_cbn_intervention_fund.vitics_meaning: AEDC drew nearly NGN25 billion from CBN's sector bailout fund. This is government money backstopping a private company to pay its electricity supplier (NBET, also government). The money is circular: government funds AEDC to pay government-owned bulk trader. Private shareholders collect management fees throughout.

section_15_nelmco_transition_liabilities.pre_completion_receivables: All trade receivables as at 31 October 2013 transferred to NELMCO without recourse. Includes cash held at privatisation date.
section_15_nelmco_transition_liabilities.pre_completion_liabilities: All liabilities and contingent liabilities as at 31 October 2013 transferred to NELMCO.
section_15_nelmco_transition_liabilities.reconciliation_status: As at August 2020, reconciliation between AEDC and NELMCO not yet completed.
section_15_nelmco_transition_liabilities.NELMCO_loan_original: NGN1.21bn loan for letter of credit collateral, 10% over 48 months

cash_balance_dispute.background: NERC/NELMCO correspondence in 2014 indicated AEDC may owe NELMCO cash-in-bank at privatisation date
cash_balance_dispute.resolution: Agreement reached in 2018 — NGN1.56bn agreed as payable. NGN325.64m excess written back to P&L.

section_15_nelmco_transition_liabilities.vitics_meaning: Six years after privatisation, the transition liabilities with NELMCO remain unreconciled. The pre-2013 debt tail still sits unresolved. This is a fundamental unfinished business of Nigeria's electricity privatisation.

CAPMI_historical.scheme_description: Credited Advance Payment for Metering Implementation — consumers advanced meter costs at 12% nominal interest, repayable over 3 years via electricity units
CAPMI_historical.note: CAPMI abolished but balance still outstanding. Consumers who pre-paid for meters in 2015-2016 are still waiting for full repayment via electricity credit.

section_16_customer_contributed_assets.USAID_asset_note: In 2018, AEDC received a specialised streetlight and line maintenance vehicle from USAID — included in government grant deferred income.
section_16_customer_contributed_assets.accounting_treatment: Customer assets recognised at fair value as PPE. Revenue recognised immediately where no future performance obligation, or as deferred income released over time as obligation discharged.
section_16_customer_contributed_assets.cumulative_pattern_note: 2015: consumers gave NGN127m in infrastructure. 2019: consumers gave NGN2.76bn in meters and a substation. The pattern is consistent — in a system that cannot fund itself, consumers become the involuntary infrastructure investors.

section_17_grid_defection_signals.scoring_rationale: Score upgraded from 3/10 in 2015 to 5/10 in 2019 — the structural conditions for mass solar adoption are now fully present.

quantitative_signals.cash_collapse: Cash fell from NGN4.4bn to NGN1.3bn — utility cannot fund operations
quantitative_signals.impairment_growth: Impairment on receivables grew 14% year-on-year — collection resistance increasing

behavioural_signals.prepaid_shift: Prepaid share growing — consumers demanding control over consumption, rejecting estimated bills
behavioural_signals.MAP_participation: NGN2.63bn of consumer-paid meters — consumers investing in their own metering, building infrastructure skills
behavioural_signals.KANN_collapse: Majority owner enters receivership January 2020 — ultimate trust destruction event. FCT consumers learn their DISCO is owned by a company that cannot pay its debts.
behavioural_signals.licence_cancellation_threat: October 2019 — NERC publicly threatens to cancel AEDC licence. Consumers watching

external_context_2019.Solar_Power_Naija_launched: 2020 (announced 2019) — government's biggest solar programme. AEDC financial crisis makes the solar alternative more attractive.
external_context_2019.off_grid_market: By 2019, solar market in Nigeria is commercially visible and growing
external_context_2019.exchange_rate: NGN361.66/USD average 2019 — solar panels still dollar-denominated, limiting some access
external_context_2019.fuel_subsidy_still_in_place: Subsidy removal not yet (comes 2023) — diesel generators still somewhat affordable

section_17_grid_defection_signals.vitics_narrative: In 2019, AEDC consumers in FCT, Niger, Kogi and Nasarawa States are experiencing: seven years of continuous losses at their DISCO, estimated bills they cannot verify, a majority owner about to collapse, a regulator threatening to cancel the licence, cash that keeps declining, and infrastructure they increasingly fund themselves. The grid is not failing suddenly — it is failing in slow motion. Every consumer who can afford a solar panel has every rational reason to defect. The 2020 Solar Power Naija programme will land into this exact environment.

section_18_consumer_impact.score_rationale: Upgraded from 7/10 in 2015. Six years of continuous losses, KANN about to collapse, licence cancellation threatened, cash nearly gone, estimated billing still dominant.

direct_impacts.estimated_billing: Majority still unmetered — bills calculated by load wattage formula, not actual consumption. Loss rate on unmetered low-tier residential: 71%.
direct_impacts.tariff_structure: Consumers paying regulated tariffs that do not cover costs. The gap is real electricity system poverty.
direct_impacts.meter_funding: Consumers paying full cost of their own meters through MAP scheme — no government or DISCO subsidy.
direct_impacts.collections: NIBOR+4% interest accruing on AEDC's unpaid bills — this cost eventually passes to consumers through tariff increases.
direct_impacts.service_quality: No service quality data published in this report. Infrastructure capex below depreciation — quality degrading.
direct_impacts.licence_threat: Consumers in October 2019 faced possibility that their distribution company could lose its licence — meaning no legal electricity supply

indirect_search_signals: how to get prepaid meter Abuja
AEDC estimated bill how to change
how to stop NEPA estimated billing

consumer_trust_events_2019: October 2019: NERC publicly threatens AEDC licence cancellation — consumers learn their DISCO may not legally exist
KANN receivership January 2020: consumers learn their DISCO's owner cannot pay its own debts
COVID-19 March 2020: AEDC exempted from lockdown but demand falls, collections worsen

section_19_five_year_trajectory.equity_trajectory_note: AEDC's equity went from positive NGN36.4bn in 2015 (at privatisation minus 2 years) to negative NGN128.8bn by 2018 — a destruction of NGN165bn in shareholder value in 3 years. The 2019 'recovery' to negative NGN4.6bn is entirely due to accounting adjustments from NERC awards, not real recovery.
section_19_five_year_trajectory.revenue_spike_note: 2019 revenue of NGN256bn vs 2018 NGN82bn — a 211% increase that is almost entirely the one-off NERC shortfall award. Real electricity sales grew only 7.5%.
section_19_five_year_trajectory.liquidity_trajectory: Net current liabilities worsened every year 2015-2018, improved 2019 only due to NERC netting reducing NBET payables. The underlying operational liquidity is deteriorating.
section_19_five_year_trajectory.vitics_reading: The five-year data tells the true story: AEDC has been a zombie utility since at least 2016. Losses of NGN327 billion over 5 years (before 2019 adjustment). The company exists because: (1) it cannot legally be shut down without a replacement, (2) government keeps injecting cash and forgiving debts, (3) consumers fund their own infrastructure, and (4) NBET forbears. When one of these props fails — as KANN did in 2020 — the system reveals its fragility.

vitics_analysis_instructions.primary_signal: AEDC 2019 is the inflection point document. Five years of accumulated failure crystallised into one year. The NERC shortfall award, the KANN receivership (post year-end), the licence cancellation threat, the MAP consumer-funded meters, the cash collapse — all in one year.
key_questions_for_youtube_analysis: Did Nigerian YouTube search volume for 'solar Abuja', 'inverter FCT', 'off-grid electricity Nasarawa' increase in 2019-2020?
Did consumers in AEDC coverage areas discuss KANN receivership and its implications for electricity supply?
How did Solar Power Naija 2020 land in communities already conditioned by AEDC's visible failure?
Was there a correlation between AEDC coverage states and solar adoption velocity?

comparison_with_2015.summary: Every metric is worse in 2019 than 2015 except the accounting profit line, which is artificial. The conditions for grid defection that were 'building' in 2015 are now fully present in 2019.

transition_markers.MAP_as_consumer_infrastructure_moment: In 2019, consumers began systematically funding their own meter infrastructure through MAP. This is the most significant behavioural shift in the document. A consumer who has paid for their own meter has already started the psychological journey to owning their own energy.
transition_markers.prepaid_growth_as_defection_precursor: 35% growth in prepaid revenue represents consumers taking control of their electricity consumption decisions. Prepaid is a gateway behaviour — the consumer who switches to prepaid is the consumer who later considers solar.
transition_markers.KANN_collapse_as_trust_destruction: When your electricity company's owner goes into receivership, the social contract of grid electricity is broken at a fundamental level.

document_quality.reading_method: Full text extraction from PDF — all financial notes, narrative sections, five-year summary, and post-balance sheet events read
document_quality.confidence_level: HIGH — clean IFRS-compliant document, all figures cross-referenced across statements
document_quality.key_document_peculiarity: Report signed August 2020 for December 2019 year-end. Contains post-balance sheet events including COVID-19, tariff suspension, and KANN receivership — making it unusually rich as a forward-looking signal document.
document_quality.auditor_opinion: Unqualified but with going concern emphasis of matter
